serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving consists of two slices of white bread (approximately 80 g total) spread with fruit jam, commonly consumed at breakfast or as a snack.

nutrient_source.title: White bread with jam — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for white bread with jam (cr_id F05020126). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 75 is based on white bread GI values published in the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI and GL Values (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). White bread consistently shows GI ~70–75 in multiple peer-reviewed studies. Jam adds concentrated sugar but does not substantially alter the GI of the bread component. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (75 × 40.14) / 100 ≈ 30.1, based on 80 g serving providing approximately 40.14 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 369 mg/100 g with low potassium and fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Sodium content of 369 mg/100 g is in the borderline-high range; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, choose low-sodium bread varieties, and replace jam with unsweetened spreads to reduce both sodium and sugar-driven insulin-mediated sodium retention.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (75), high carbohydrate load (50.2 g/100 g), and low dietary fibre (2.28 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: White bread with jam has a high glycaemic index of 75 and delivers a glycaemic load of approximately 30 per serving; individuals with type 2 diabetes should substitute wholegrain or high-fibre bread, use no-added-sugar jam, and pair with a protein source to blunt postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate dominance, elevated sodium, and low fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of refined white bread with jam contributes to elevated triglycerides and LDL-C through high refined-carbohydrate intake (50.2 g/100 g) and borderline sodium (369 mg/100 g); replacing with wholegrain bread and reducing jam quantity is recommended for cardiovascular risk management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined carbohydrates and added sugar promote pro-inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: White bread with jam provides a high refined-carbohydrate and added-sugar load with no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should replace with wholegrain bread and a polyphenol-rich spread to reduce pro-inflammatory cytokine activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: White bread with jam is a simple, widely consumed bread-based dish consisting of sliced white bread spread with fruit jam. The white bread is made from refined wheat flour, yielding a soft, light crumb with a mild flavour and a slightly chewy texture. Fruit jam contributes sweetness and a fruity flavour profile. Per 100 g, this combination provides 249 kcal, 8.04 g protein, 2.3 g fat, 50.17 g carbohydrate, 9.92 g sugar, 2.28 g dietary fibre, 369 mg sodium, 177 mg calcium, and 4.5 mg iron. The glycaemic index is high at approximately 75, driven by the refined flour base of white bread, and the glycaemic load per typical 80 g serving is approximately 30, making it a high-glycaemic meal choice. Clinically, this food is unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to rapid postprandial glucose elevation, borderline concerning for hypertension due to sodium content, and pro-inflammatory for rheumatoid arthritis due to refined carbohydrate and added sugar content. It is commonly consumed at breakfast or as a snack and is widely available across Singapore, Western, and international food contexts. Substituting wholegrain bread and no-added-sugar jam significantly improves the nutritional profile.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020126) with a reported confidence of 0.95. GI value of 75 is consistent with multiple peer-reviewed publications and the University of Sydney GI Database for white bread. All core macros and key secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite white bread with jam entry are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database record F05020126. The input description references chocolate spread and peanut butter, but the HPB nutrient data and the canonical name correspond to white bread with fruit jam; the record has been processed as white bread with jam per the canonical HPB entry. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate based on white bread GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI and GL Values, Diabetes Care. GL per serving calculated as (75 × 40.14 g carbohydrate per 80 g serving) / 100 ≈ 30.1. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule is not triggered.
